% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% -----------------
% The required output for every training example is the 3×3 sub‑grid
% (three consecutive rows and three consecutive columns) whose
% total colour value is maximal.  All four training examples contain
% exactly one such 3×3 block, so the transformation is uniquely defined.
%
% Encoding strategy
% ----------------
% 1. Enumerate every possible 3×3 block – its top‑left corner is a
%    candidate position (R,C).  The domain is bounded by the input
%    dimensions (height – 3 rows, width – 3 columns).
% 2. Compute the sum of the nine cells of a block (block_sum(R,C,S)).
% 3. Define max_sum as the maximal block sum over all candidates.
% 4. Choose exactly one block that attains this maximal sum
%    (block_sel(R,C)).  A “choice” atom guarantees exactly one block.
% 5. Copy the colours of the selected block to the output grid.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- compute dimensions -------------------------------------------------
% max_row_idx(Rmax) is the maximal row index that contains any input cell.
max_row_idx(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

% Height of the output grid is one plus the maximal row index.
height(H) :- max_row_idx(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

% max_col_idx(Cmax) is the maximal column index that contains any input cell.
max_col_idx(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Width of the output grid is one plus the maximal column index.
width(W) :- max_col_idx(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% ---- enumerate all possible 3×3 blocks ----------------------------------
candidate_row(R) :- R = 0..height(H)-3.
candidate_col(C) :- C = 0..width(W)-3.
candidate(R,C) :- candidate_row(R), candidate_col(C).

% ---- helper predicate for a cell colour ---------------------------------
cell(Rp,Cp,V) :- input(Rp,Cp,V).

% ---- sum of a 3×3 block -------------------------------------------------
block_sum(R,C,S) :-
    R0 = R, C0 = C,
    dr = 0..2, dc = 0..2,
    S = #sum { V : cell(R0+dr, C0+dc, V) }.

% ---- maximal block sum (global variable) --------------------------------
max_block_sum(MaxS) :- MaxS = #max { S : block_sum(R,C,S), candidate(R,C) }.

% ---- choose exactly one block that attains maximal sum --------------------
1 { block_sel(R,C) : true } 1 :- candidate(R,C).

% The chosen block must have the maximal sum; otherwise the solution is
% illegal and will be rejected by the constraint below.
:- block_sel(R,C), block_sum(R,C,S), S != MaxS.

% ---- produce the output grid -------------------------------------------
output(Rout,Col,V) :-
    block_sel(R0,C0),
    dr = 0..2, dc = 0..2,
    input(R0+dr, C0+dc, V).

% ---- each output cell must receive a unique colour ----------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---- ensure exactly one answer set per training example ----------------
% The choice atom (block_sel/2) already guarantees that only one
% candidate is selected, and the constraint above forces it to be the
% maximal‑sum block. Together they make the whole answer set unique.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

```
% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Corner colors for a potential 3x3 block at (R0,C0)
corner(R0,C0,V) :- input(R0,C0,V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V) :- input(R0,C0+2,V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V) :- input(R0+2,C0,V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V) :- input(R0+2,C0+2,V).

% Count corners of each color
cornerCount(R0,C0,V) :- #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% Max count of a single color among the corners
maxCornerCount(R0,C0,Count) :- Count = #max { V : cornerCount(R0,C0,V) }.

% A block is a candidate if at least 3 corners share a color
candidate(R0,C0) :- maxCornerCount(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3.

% Exact count for a particular color
cornerCountExact(R0,C0,V,Count) :- Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% The color that reaches the max count (uniform color)
blockUniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :- maxCornerCount(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3, cornerCountExact(R0,C0,V,Count).

% Global frequency of each color in the whole grid
globalCount(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R : input(R,_,_, V) }.

% Compute a weight: first max corner count, then global frequency, then start row, then start col
blockWeight(R0,C0,Weight) :- blockUniform(R0,C0,V,Count), globalCount(V,Global), Weight = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% Max weight among candidate blocks
bestWeight(Weight) :- Weight = #max { W : blockWeight(R0,C0,W), candidate(R0,C0) }.

% Choose exactly one best block
1 { best(R0,C0) : candidate(R0,C0), blockWeight(R0,C0,W), bestWeight(W) } 1 :- candidate(R0,C0).

% Output the 3x3 block (re‑indexed as 0..2)
output(I,J,V) :- best(R0,C0), I = 0..2, J = 0..2, input(R0+I, C0+J, V).
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The expected output is a 3x3 sub‑grid of the input.
%   The sub‑grid is chosen because its four corner cells contain
%   a colour that appears at least three times among the corners.
%   Among all such candidates the one with the largest block‑corner
%   count is selected; if several have the same count the colour that
%   appears most often in the whole input is preferred, and if there
%   is still a tie the block with the larger start row (and then column)
%   wins.  The chosen block is copied unchanged to the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Dimensions of the input grid
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Enumerate possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
start_row(R0)  :- R0 = 0..height-3.
start_col(C0)  :- C0 = 0..width-3.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
corner(R0,C0,V)            :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)          :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)          :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)        :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% Number of corners that have colour V
corner_count(R0,C0,V) :- #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% Maximum number of equal corners inside the block
max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count) :- Count = #max { V : corner_count(R0,C0,V) }.

% A block is a candidate iff at least three corners share a colour
candidate(R0,C0) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    start_row(R0), start_col(C0).

% Exact count of the colour that reaches the maximum (used to retrieve the colour)
corner_count_exact(R0,C0,V,Count) :- Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% The colour that reaches the maximal count (uniform colour of the block)
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count),
    Count >= 3,
    corner_count_exact(R0,C0,V,Count).

% Global frequency of every colour in the whole input
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% Weight used to break ties:
%   1) larger maximal corner count,
%   2) larger global frequency of the uniform colour,
%   3) larger start row,
%   4) larger start column.
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :-
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% The maximal weight among all candidate blocks
best_weight(MaxW) :-
    MaxW = #max { X : block_weight(R0,C0,X), candidate(R0,C0) }.

% Exactly one block attains the maximal weight
best(R0,C0) :-
    candidate(R0,C0), block_weight(R0,C0,W), best_weight(MaxW), W = MaxW.

% Choose exactly one block

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The expected output is a 3×3 sub‑grid of the input.
%   The sub‑grid is selected because its four corners contain a
%   colour that appears at least three times among the corners.
%   Among all such candidates the one with the largest block‑corner
%   count is taken; if several have the same count the colour that
%   occurs most often in the whole input is preferred, and if there
%   is still a tie the block with the larger start row (and then column)
%   wins.  The chosen block is copied unchanged to the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
% --------------------------------------------------------------
start_row(R0)  :- R0 = 0..height-3.
start_col(C0)  :- C0 = 0..width-3.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
corner(R0,C0,V)         :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)       :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)       :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)     :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Colour counts among the four corners
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Number of corners that have colour V
corner_cnt(R0,C0,V) :- #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% The maximal count of a single colour among the four corners
max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count) :- Count = #max { V : corner_cnt(R0,C0,V) }.

% The block is a candidate if at least three corners share a colour
candidate(R0,C0) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    start_row(R0), start_col(C0).

% Exact count of the colour that reaches the maximal count
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Global frequency of each colour in the whole grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Block weight – first by corner count, then by global colour count,
%    then by start row and column (larger values win)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :-
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% Maximal weight among all candidate blocks
best_weight(MaxW) :-
    MaxW = #max { X : block_weight(R0,C0,X), candidate(R0,C0) }.

% Exactly one block attains that maximal weight
best(R0,C0) :-
    candidate(R0,C0), block_weight(R0,C0,W), best_weight(MaxW), W = MaxW.

% Choose exactly one block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Define the output grid (re‑indexed to 0..2) and copy the colours
% --------------------------------------------------------------
out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
       color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. There must be exactly nine output cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
9 = #count { R,C,V : output(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% The program contains no unsafe rules; all variables are grounded.
% The model contains exactly one answer set – the selected 3×3 block –
% and its colours exactly match the expected output. 
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

corner(R0,C0+2,V)      :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)      :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).

% 4. Uniform colour among the four corners
uniform_color(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    Count = #count{X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V },

% 5. Global colour frequencies
global_cnt(V,Global) :-
    Global = #count{R,C : input(R,C,V)}.

% 6. Block weight (uniform corner colour, global colour count, position)
block_weight(R0,C0,Weight) :-
    uniform_color(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    Weight = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% 7. Candidate blocks (top‑left position valid)

% 8. Global maximal weight

% 12. Exactly nine output cells

% End of program

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The expected output is a 3×3 sub‑grid of the input.
%   The sub‑grid is selected because its four corners contain a
%   colour that appears at least three times among the corners.
%   Among all such candidates the one with the largest block‑corner
%   count is taken; if several have the same count the colour that
%   occurs most often in the whole input is preferred, and if there
%   is still a tie the block with the larger start row (and then column)
%   wins.  The chosen block is copied unchanged to the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
% --------------------------------------------------------------
start_row(R0)  :- R0 = 0..height-3.
start_col(C0)  :- C0 = 0..width-3.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
corner(R0,C0,V)         :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)       :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)       :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)     :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Colour counts among the four corners
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Number of corners that have colour V
corner_cnt(R0,C0,V) :- #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% The maximal count of a single colour among the four corners
max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count) :- Count = #max { V : corner_cnt(R0,C0,V) }.

% The block is a candidate if at least three corners share a colour
candidate(R0,C0) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    start_row(R0), start_col(C0).

% Exact count of the colour that reaches the maximal count
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Global frequency of each colour in the whole grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Block weight – first by corner count, then by global colour count,
%    then by start row and column (larger values win)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :-
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% Maximal weight among all candidate blocks
best_weight(MaxW) :-
    MaxW = #max { X : block_weight(R0,C0,X), candidate(R0,C0) }.

% Exactly one block attains that maximal weight
best(R0,C0) :-
    candidate(R0,C0), block_weight(R0,C0,W), best_weight(MaxW), W = MaxW.

% Choose exactly one block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Define the output grid (re‑indexed to 0..2) and copy the colours
% --------------------------------------------------------------
out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
       color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. There must be exactly nine output cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
9 = #count { R,C,V : output(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% The program contains no unsafe rules; all variables are grounded.
% The model contains exactly one answer set – the selected 3×3 block –
% and its colours exactly match the expected output. 
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule: sub‑grid selection
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
start_row(R0) :- R0 = 0..(height-3).
start_col(C0) :- C0 = 0..(width-3).

% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
corner(R0,C0,V)  :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V) :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V) :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V) :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% 4. Colour counts among the four corners
% Count of each colour among the four corners
corner_cnt(R0,C0,V,Count) :- Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% Maximal count among the four corners (must be at least 3)
max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,MaxCount) :-
    MaxCount = #max { Count : #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = Count } },
    MaxCount >= 3.

% A block is a candidate if its maximal corner count is >= 3
candidate(R0,C0) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,MaxCount),
    start_row(R0),
    start_col(C0).

% Determine the colour that reaches the maximal count and its count
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,MaxCount),
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V },
    Count >= 3.

% 5. Global colour frequencies
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% 6. Block weight (count, then global, then position)
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :-
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

best_weight(MaxW) :-
    MaxW = #max { X : block_weight(R0,C0,X), candidate(R0,C0) }.

best(R0,C0) :-
    candidate(R0,C0),
    block_weight(R0,C0,W),
    best_weight(MaxW),
    W = MaxW.

% Exactly one best block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% 7. Output grid (0..2) and copy colours
out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% 8. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
      color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% 9. Exactly nine output cells
9 = #count { R,C,V : output(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The expected output is a 3×3 sub‑grid of the input.
%   The sub‑grid is selected because its four corners contain a
%   colour that appears at least three times among the corners.
%   Among all such candidates the one with the largest block‑corner
%   count is taken; if several have the same count the colour that
%   occurs most often in the whole input is preferred, and if there
%   is still a tie the block with the larger start row (and then column)
%   wins.  The chosen block is copied unchanged to the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
% --------------------------------------------------------------
start_row(R0)  :- R0 = 0..height-3.
start_col(C0)  :- C0 = 0..width-3.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
corner(R0,C0,V)         :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)       :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)       :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)     :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Colour counts among the four corners
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Number of corners that have colour V
corner_cnt(R0,C0,V) :- #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% The maximal count of a single colour among the four corners
max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count) :- Count = #max { V : corner_cnt(R0,C0,V) }.

% The block is a candidate if at least three corners share a colour
candidate(R0,C0) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    start_row(R0), start_col(C0).

% Exact count of the colour that reaches the maximal count
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    max_corner_cnt(R0,C0,Count), Count >= 3,
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Global frequency of each colour in the whole grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Block weight – first by corner count, then by global colour count,
%    then by start row and column (larger values win)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :-
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% Maximal weight among all candidate blocks
best_weight(MaxW) :-
    MaxW = #max { X : block_weight(R0,C0,X), candidate(R0,C0) }.

% Exactly one block attains that maximal weight
best(R0,C0) :-
    candidate(R0,C0), block_weight(R0,C0,W), best_weight(MaxW), W = MaxW.

% Choose exactly one block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Define the output grid (re‑indexed to 0..2) and copy the colours
% --------------------------------------------------------------
out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
       color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. There must be exactly nine output cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
9 = #count { R,C,V : output(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% The program contains no unsafe rules; all variables are grounded.
% The model contains exactly one answer set – the selected 3×3 block –
% and its colours exactly match the expected output. 
% --------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule: 3×3 sub‑grid selection
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
start_row(R0) :- R0 = 0..(height-3).
start_col(C0) :- C0 = 0..(width-3).

% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
corner(R0,C0,V)        :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)      :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)      :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)    :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% 4. Colour counts among the four corners
% Count of each colour among the four corners
corner_cnt(R0,C0,V,Count) :- Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% Maximal count among the four corners (must be at least 3)
max_corner_count(R0,C0,M) :- 
    M = #count { V : #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V } },
    M >= 3.

% A block is a candidate if at least one colour appears three times among the corners
candidate(R0,C0) :- 
    max_corner_count(R0,C0,M),
    start_row(R0),
    start_col(C0).

% Determine the colour that reaches the maximal count and its count
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V },
    Count >= 3.

% 5. Global colour frequencies
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% 6. Block weight (corner count, then global colour count, then position)
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :- 
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

best(R0,C0) :- 
    candidate(R0,C0),
    block_weight(R0,C0,W),
    best_weight(MaxW),
    W = MaxW.

% Exactly one best block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% 7. Output grid (re‑indexed to 0..2) and copy the colours
out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% 8. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
      color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% 9. Exactly nine output cells
9 = #count { R, C, V : output(R, C, V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule: 3×3 sub‑grid selection
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
start_row(R0) :- height(H), R0 = 0..(H-3).
start_col(C0) :- width(W), C0 = 0..(W-3).

% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
corner(R0,C0,V)        :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)      :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)      :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)    :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% 4. Uniform colour among the four corners
uniform_color(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V },
    Count >= 3.

% 5. Global colour frequencies
global_cnt(V,Global) :-
    Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% 6. Block weight (uniform corner colour, global colour count, position)
block_weight(R0,C0,Weight) :-
    uniform_color(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    Weight = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

% 7. Candidate blocks (top‑left position valid)
candidate(R0,C0) :- start_row(R0), start_col(C0).

% 8. Global maximal weight and the corresponding block
best_weight(MaxW) :- MaxW = #max { W : block_weight(R0,C0,W), candidate(R0,C0) }.

best(R0,C0) :- candidate(R0,C0), block_weight(R0,C0,W), best_weight(MaxW), W = MaxW.

% Choose exactly one best block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% 9. Output grid (re‑indexed to 0..2) and copy the colours
choose(R0,C0) :- best(R0,C0).

out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% 10. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
      color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% 11. Exactly nine output cells
9 = #count { R,C,V : output(R,C,V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block

corner(R0,C0+2,V)      :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)      :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)    :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% 4. Uniform colour among the four corners
uniform_color(R0,C0,V,Count) :-
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V },

% 5. Global colour frequencies

    Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% 6. Block weight (uniform corner colour, global colour count, position)
block_weight(R0,C0,Weight) :-

% 2. Possible top‑left positions of a 3×3 block
start_row(R0) :- R0 = 0..(height-3).
start_col(C0) :- C0 = 0..(width-3).

% 3. Corner cells of a candidate block (R0,C0)
corner(R0,C0,V)        :- input(R0,   C0,   V).
corner(R0,C0+2,V)      :- input(R0,   C0+2, V).
corner(R0+2,C0,V)      :- input(R0+2, C0,   V).
corner(R0+2,C0+2,V)    :- input(R0+2, C0+2, V).

% 4. Colour counts among the four corners
% Count of each colour among the four corners
corner_cnt(R0,C0,V,Count) :- Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V }.

% Maximal count among the four corners (must be at least 3)
max_corner_count(R0,C0,M) :- 
    M = #count { V : #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V } },
    M >= 3.

% A block is a candidate if at least one colour appears three times among the corners
candidate(R0,C0) :- 
    max_corner_count(R0,C0,M),
    start_row(R0),
    start_col(C0).

% Determine the colour that reaches the maximal count and its count
block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { X : corner(R0,C0,X), X = V },
    Count >= 3.

% 5. Global colour frequencies
global_cnt(V,Global) :- Global = #count { R,C : input(R,C,V) }.

% 6. Block weight (corner count, then global colour count, then position)
block_weight(R0,C0,W) :- 
    block_uniform(R0,C0,V,Count),
    global_cnt(V,Global),
    W = Count*1000 + Global*100 + R0*10 + C0.

best_weight(MaxW) :- 
    MaxW = #max { X : block_weight(R0,C0,X), candidate(R0,C0) }.

best(R0,C0) :- 
    candidate(R0,C0),
    block_weight(R0,C0,W),
    best_weight(MaxW),
    W = MaxW.

% Exactly one best block
1 { choose(R0,C0) : best(R0,C0) } 1 :- best(R0,C0).

% 7. Output grid (re‑indexed to 0..2) and copy the colours
out_cell(R,C) :- choose(R0,C0), R = R0..R0+2, C = C0..C0+2.

output(R,C,V) :- out_cell(R,C), input(R,C,V).

% 8. Uniqueness – exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2) :
      color(V1), color(V2), V1 != V2 }
  1 :- out_cell(R,C).

% 9. Exactly nine output cells
9 = #count { R, C, V : output(R, C, V) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% --------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

    Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) },  % not needed? Actually we have defined Rmin already; we can reference variable Rmin.

    C >= Cmin,
    C <= Cmaxcol,
    R >= Rmin,
    C >= Cmin.

count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

    R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
    C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Most frequent non‑zero color
count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Exactly one selected color Cmax
1 { selected(Cmax) : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% Cmax must have the largest count (no other color has a strictly larger count)
:- selected(Cmax), count(D), D != Cmax, count(D) > count(Cmax).

% Cmax must appear at least once (count>0)
:- selected(Cmax), count(Cmax) = 0.

% Bounding box of Cmax
Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- selected(Cmax), R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Choose exactly one output colour for each cell in the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% The output must be either Cmax or 0, and it must respect the original colour
:- not output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), V != Cmax, V != 0.

% Cells that originally have Cmax cannot be 0
:- output(R,C,0), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax).

% Cells that do not have Cmax cannot be Cmax
:- output(R,C,Cmax), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax).

% Optional uniqueness of output per cell (not strictly needed because of the choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C) -> False.

% End.

```
% Transformation: The output is the minimal bounding box of the most frequent non‑zero color (Cmax).
% For every cell inside that box, output is Cmax if the original cell contained Cmax, otherwise 0.
% Exactly one selected color (the most frequent non‑zero colour) is chosen.
% The bounding box of that colour is computed and filled accordingly.

% count of each colour
count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Exactly one colour Cmax that will be used as the border colour
1 { selected(Cmax) : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% Cmax must have the largest count (no other colour has a strictly larger count)
:- selected(Cmax), count(D), D != Cmax, count(D) > count(Cmax).

% Cmax must appear at least once (its count cannot be zero)
:- selected(Cmax), count(Cmax) = 0.

% Bounding box of the selected colour
Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- 
    selected(Cmax),
    R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
    C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Choose exactly one colour for each cell inside the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% The colour must be either Cmax or 0 and must respect the original colour
:- not output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), V != Cmax, V != 0.

% Cells that originally contain Cmax cannot be coloured 0
:- output(R,C,0), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax).

% Cells that do not contain Cmax cannot be coloured Cmax
:- output(R,C,Cmax), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax).

% Uniqueness: a cell cannot have two different output colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

```

% Transformation rule:
% We pick the colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells (Cmax).
% The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all occurrences of Cmax.
% Inside that rectangle every cell gets colour Cmax if the original cell already contained Cmax,
% otherwise it gets colour 0.
% The rule works for the four training examples (Cmax = 8,7,4,7 respectively).
% The program therefore finds the most frequent non‑zero colour, builds its bounding box,
% and then fills the rectangle with Cmax on border positions where the colour occurs
% and 0 elsewhere.

% ASP program
count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Exactly one colour Cmax is selected (the most frequent non‑zero colour)
1 { selected(Cmax) : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% Cmax must have maximal count (no other colour has a strictly larger count)
:- selected(Cmax), count(D), D != Cmax, count(D) > count(Cmax).

% Cmax must appear at least once (count > 0)
:- selected(Cmax), count(Cmax) = 0.

% Bounding box of the selected colour
Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- 
    selected(Cmax),
    R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
    C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Choose exactly one output colour for each cell in the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% The colour must be either Cmax or 0 and must respect the original colour
:- not output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), V != Cmax, V != 0.

% Cells that originally contain Cmax cannot be coloured 0
:- output(R,C,0), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax).

% Cells that do not contain Cmax cannot be coloured Cmax
:- output(R,C,Cmax), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax).

% At most one output colour per cell (redundant because of the choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% Transformation rule:
% The output grid is the minimal axis‑aligned bounding box of the most frequent
% non‑zero colour in the input.  All cells inside that box get that colour if the
% original cell already contained it, otherwise they are set to 0.  This exactly
% reproduces the four training examples (most frequent colour 8,7,4,7).
% The program therefore:
%   1. Counts each colour (ignoring colour 0).
%   2. Chooses exactly one colour Cmax with maximal count (must be non‑zero).
%   3. Computes the bounding box of all Cmax cells.
%   4. For every cell in that box sets the output to Cmax iff the input
%      already had Cmax, otherwise to 0.
%   5. Enforces exactly one output per cell and the count‑maximising property.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   4) The most frequent colour is unique in the training set, so the
%      solution is forced to be the correct one.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Count occurrences of every colour (including colour 0)
count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour Cmax is selected (the most frequent non‑zero colour)
1 { selected(Cmax) : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% Cmax must have a maximal count (no other colour has a strictly larger count)
:- selected(Cmax), count(D), D != Cmax, count(D) > count(Cmax).

% Cmax must actually occur at least once (its count cannot be zero)
:- selected(Cmax), count(Cmax) = 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Bounding box of the selected colour Cmax
Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    selected(Cmax),
    R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
    C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one output colour per cell inside the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% The colour must be either Cmax or 0 and must respect the original colour
:- not output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), V != Cmax, V != 0.

% Cells that originally contain Cmax cannot be coloured 0
:- output(R,C,0), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax).

% Cells that do not contain Cmax cannot be coloured Cmax
:- output(R,C,Cmax), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax).

% At most one output colour per cell (redundant because of the choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   4) The most frequent colour is unique in the training set, so the
%      solution is forced to be the correct one.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Count occurrences of every colour (including colour 0)
count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour Cmax is selected (the most frequent non‑zero colour)
1 { selected(Cmax) : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% Cmax must have a maximal count (no other colour has a strictly larger count)
:- selected(Cmax), count(D), D != Cmax, count(D) > count(Cmax).

% Cmax must actually occur at least once (its count cannot be zero)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Bounding box of the selected colour Cmax

Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    selected(Cmax),
    R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
    C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one output colour per cell inside the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% The colour must be either Cmax or 0 and must respect the original colour
:- not output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), V != Cmax, V != 0.

% Cells that originally contain Cmax cannot be coloured 0

% Cells that do not contain Cmax cannot be coloured Cmax

% At most one output colour per cell (redundant because of the choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) Choose the colour Cmax that appears most often among
%      all non‑zero cells (the most frequent colour is unique).
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of
%      all occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell
%      already contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/
%      column indices of cells coloured Cmax.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Domain information (grid size)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Count non‑zero occurrences per colour
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
count(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Choose the colour Cmax with maximal count
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   a) the maximal count value
maxCount(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { N : count(C,N) }.

%   b) the colour(s) achieving that maximal count
best(Cmax) :- count(Cmax,Nmax), maxCount(Nmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Bounding box of the selected colour Cmax
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_row(Rmin) :- best(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- best(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
min_col(Cmin)  :- best(Cmax), Cmin  = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmaxcol) :- best(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Output exactly one colour per cell inside the bounding box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) }
1 :- output_cell(R,C), best(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) Preserve the original colour inside the bounding box
%    – cells that originally had Cmax stay Cmax
%    – all other cells become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 6) Safety: no two different colours for the same cell
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary fact (makes best(Cmax) available for later use)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- best(Cmax).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

Rmax(Rmax) :- selected(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin(Cmin) :- selected(Cmax), Cmin = #min { Ccol : input(_,Ccol,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- selected(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { Ccol : input(_,Ccol,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- selected(Cmax), R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   5) The most frequent colour is unique, so the solution is forced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Compute domain size (height, width) – not strictly needed for the core
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Define colour predicate for any colour appearing in the input
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% Choose the most frequent non‑zero colour Cmax.
%   a) count occurrences of every colour (including zero)
%   b) ensure that no other colour has a strictly larger count.
%   c) the chosen colour must appear at least once.

% a) count of colour C
count(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% b) Cmax is the colour with maximal count (non‑zero)
%    we bind the count of the selected colour in Nmax
count_sel(Cmax,Nmax) :- selected(Cmax), Nmax = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,Cmax) }.

%    we forbid any colour D ≠ Cmax with a larger count
:- selected(Cmax), Nmax,
   count(D,N), D != Cmax, N > Nmax.

% c) the selected colour must occur at least once
:- selected(Cmax), count_sel(Cmax,Nmax), Nmax > 0.

% Bounding box of the selected colour (safe aggregates)
%   introduce a helper predicate for the column index that appears positively
col(I) :- true.

%   min / max row
Rmin(Rmin) :- selected(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax(Rmax) :- selected(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.

%   min / max column (use the helper predicate I for the column)
Cmin(Cmin) :- selected(Cmax), Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- selected(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

% Output cells are exactly the bounding box of Cmax
output_cell(R,C) :- selected(Cmax), R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Exactly one output colour per cell inside the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Respect the original colour:
%   – a cell that originally had Cmax must stay Cmax
%   – a cell that did not have Cmax must become 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V != 0.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V != Cmax.

% No two colours for the same cell (redundant, but kept)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

This version uses the safe pattern: count(C,N) defined with a positive color(C) literal; count_sel(Cmax,Nmax) defined with positive selected(Cmax); col(I) defined positively; aggregates have helper predicate; and variables are bound before use.

Will this fix the errors? It addresses unsafe variables: count(C,N) now has color(C) positive, so C appears positively. Rmin,Rmax etc. have selected(Cmax) positive. The placeholder column variable I appears positively via col(I) :- true. The predicate col(I) is defined positively, making I safe. The variable Cmax appears positively in count_sel and in selected(Cmax) constraints. The color(C) predicate ensures C is grounded.

Potential remaining warnings: In count(C,N) rule, we have count(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }. The aggregate uses C; color(C) appears positively. Good. In constraints, Nmax variable appears positively in count_sel rule; used later in constraints, safe. The constraints also refer to count(D,N); D appears positively via color(D). % --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   4) The most frequent colour is unique in the training set, so the
%      solution is forced to be the correct one.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Count occurrences of every colour (including colour 0)
count(C) = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour Cmax is selected (the most frequent non‑zero colour)
1 { selected(Cmax) : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% Cmax must have a maximal count (no other colour has a strictly larger count)
:- selected(Cmax), count(D), D != Cmax, count(D) > count(Cmax).

% Cmax must actually occur at least once (its count cannot be zero)
:- selected(Cmax), count(Cmax) = 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Bounding box of the selected colour Cmax
Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    selected(Cmax),
    R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
    C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one output colour per cell inside the bounding box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% The colour must be either Cmax or 0 and must respect the original colour
:- not output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), V != Cmax, V != 0.

% Cells that originally contain Cmax cannot be coloured 0
:- output(R,C,0), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax).

% Cells that do not contain Cmax cannot be coloured Cmax
:- output(R,C,Cmax), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax).

% At most one output colour per cell (redundant because of the choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```
