% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   5) The most frequent colour is unique, so the solution is forced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Basic domain information (not strictly required for the core logic)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate – grounded for every colour that appears in the input
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Choose the most frequent non‑zero colour (Cmax)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% a) Count occurrences of each colour (including colour 0)
count(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% b) Cmax is the colour with maximal count (non‑zero)
%    – bind the count of the selected colour
count_sel(Cmax,Nmax) :- selected(Cmax), Nmax = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,Cmax) }.

%    – forbid any other colour D ≠ Cmax with a larger count
:- selected(Cmax), Nmax,
   count(D,N), D != Cmax, N > Nmax.

% c) Cmax must occur at least once (its count cannot be zero)
:- selected(Cmax), count_sel(Cmax,Nmax), Nmax > 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Bounding box of the selected colour (safe aggregates)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicate that gives a positive occurrence for the column index
col(I) :- true.

% Min / max row of cells coloured Cmax
Rmin(Rmin) :- selected(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax(Rmax) :- selected(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.

% Min / max column of cells coloured Cmax
Cmin(Cmin) :- selected(Cmax), Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- selected(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

% Cell belongs to the axis‑aligned bounding box of Cmax
output_cell(R,C) :- selected(Cmax), R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Exactly one output colour per cell inside the bounding box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Respect the original colour:
%   – a cell that originally had Cmax must stay Cmax
%   – a cell that did not have Cmax must become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V != 0.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V != Cmax.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) No two colours for the same cell (redundant, kept for safety)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   5) The most frequent colour is unique, so the solution is forced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Domain information (grid size)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Choose a colour Cmax with maximal non‑zero count
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% a) Exactly one colour is selected (non‑zero)
1 { Cmax : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% b) Compute its count
count_sel(Cmax,Nmax) :- Cmax, Nmax = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,Cmax) }.

% c) Ensure no other colour has a larger count
:- count_sel(Cmax,Nmax),
   count_sel(D,N),
   D != Cmax,
   D != 0,
   N > Nmax.

% d) Ensure the selected colour appears at least once
:- count_sel(Cmax,Nmax), Nmax > 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Bounding box of Cmax (derived from its occurrences)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
Rmin(Rmin) :- Cmax, Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax(Rmax) :- Cmax, Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin(Cmin) :- Cmax, Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- Cmax, Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Output exactly one colour per cell inside the box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Preserve original colour inside the bounding box
%    – a cell that originally had Cmax stays Cmax
%    – other cells become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) Safety: no two colours for the same cell
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.

%      occurrences of Cmax.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

Cmin(Cmin) :- Cmax, Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- Cmax, Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   5) The most frequent colour is unique, so the solution is forced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Domain information (grid size)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Choose a colour Cmax with maximal non‑zero count
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% a) Exactly one colour is selected (non‑zero)
1 { Cmax : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% b) Count occurrences for any colour (non‑zero)
count_sel(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% c) Ensure the selected colour has maximal count
:- count_sel(Cmax,Nmax),
   count_sel(D,N),
   D != Cmax,
   N > Nmax.

% d) Ensure the selected colour appears at least once
:- count_sel(Cmax,Nmax), Nmax > 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Bounding box of Cmax (derived from its occurrences)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
Rmin(Rmin) :- Cmax, Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax(Rmax) :- Cmax, Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin(Cmin) :- Cmax, Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- Cmax, Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Output exactly one colour per cell inside the box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Preserve original colour inside the bounding box
%    – a cell that originally had Cmax stays Cmax
%    – other cells become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) Safety: no two colours for the same cell
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   5) The most frequent colour is unique, so the solution is forced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Basic domain information (not strictly required for the core logic)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate – grounded for every colour that appears in the input
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Choose the most frequent non‑zero colour (Cmax)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% a) Count occurrences of each colour (including colour 0)
count(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% b) Cmax is the colour with maximal count (non‑zero)
%    – bind the count of the selected colour
count_sel(Cmax,Nmax) :- selected(Cmax), Nmax = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,Cmax) }.

%    – forbid any other colour D ≠ Cmax with a larger count
:- selected(Cmax), Nmax,
   count(D,N), D != Cmax, N > Nmax.

% c) Cmax must occur at least once (its count cannot be zero)
:- selected(Cmax), count_sel(Cmax,Nmax), Nmax > 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Bounding box of the selected colour (safe aggregates)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicate that gives a positive occurrence for the column index
col(I) :- true.

% Min / max row of cells coloured Cmax
Rmin(Rmin) :- selected(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax(Rmax) :- selected(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.

% Min / max column of cells coloured Cmax
Cmin(Cmin) :- selected(Cmax), Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- selected(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

% Cell belongs to the axis‑aligned bounding box of Cmax
output_cell(R,C) :- selected(Cmax), R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Exactly one output colour per cell inside the bounding box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Respect the original colour:
%   – a cell that originally had Cmax must stay Cmax
%   – a cell that did not have Cmax must become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V != 0.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V != Cmax.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) No two colours for the same cell (redundant, kept for safety)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```
% Domain
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% Count occurrences of each color (non-zero)
count(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Choose most frequent colour (unique)
selected(Cmax) :- 
    maxN = #max { C : count(C,N) },
    #count { C : count(C,N), C != Cmax } = 0.

% Bounding box of selected colour
min_row(Cmax,Rmin) :- selected(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
max_row(Cmax,Rmax) :- selected(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
min_col(Cmax,Cmin) :- selected(Cmax), Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmax,Cmaxcol) :- selected(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Output rule: exactly one colour per cell inside box (but we will enforce later)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Preserve original colour: cells inside box keep their colour iff it is Cmax, else 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% No two different colours for same cell (redundant if previous constraints enforce)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).
```

```
% Domain
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% Count of occurrences for each colour (non‑zero)
count(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% For the selected colour Cmax, compute its maximal count Nmax
maxCount(Cmax,Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { C : count(C,N) }.

% Ensure the chosen colour is the unique maximum
:- maxCount(Cmax,Nmax),
   maxCount(D,Nd), D != Cmax, Nd > Nmax.

% Bounding box of Cmax
min_row(Cmax,Rmin) :- maxCount(Cmax,Nmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
max_row(Cmax,Rmax) :- maxCount(Cmax,Nmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
min_col(Cmax,Cmin) :- maxCount(Cmax,Nmax), Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmax,Cmaxcol) :- maxCount(Cmax,Nmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Exactly one colour (the selected Cmax) per cell inside the box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), selected(Cmax).

% Preserve original colour: cells inside the box keep Cmax, else become 0
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% No two different colours for the same cell (redundant but safe)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% The selected colour Cmax is defined for later use
selected(Cmax) :- maxCount(Cmax,Nmax).
```

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

min_col(Cmax,Cmin) :- best(Cmax), Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmax,Cmaxcol) :- best(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% Auxiliary rule to make best(Cmax) available
:- best(Cmax).

% Domain
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% Count non‑zero occurrences per colour
count(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Choose colour Cmax that has maximal count
best(Cmax) :-
    #max { C : count(C,N) } = Nmax,
    count(Cmax,N).

% Note: #max yields the maximal count value Nmax for colour Cmax.
% The rule best(Cmax) is true only if Cmax has the maximal count.
% (If there is a tie, multiple best/1 solutions exist.)

% Bounding box of Cmax
min_row(Rmin) :- best(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- best(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- best(Cmax), Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmaxcol) :- best(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% Exactly one colour (the selected best) per cell inside the box
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), best(Cmax).

% Preserve original colour inside the bounding box
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% No two different colours for the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% Auxiliary fact to make best(Cmax) available for later use (optional)
:- best(Cmax).
```

We'll produce final program as:

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) Choose the colour Cmax that appears most often among
%      all non‑zero cells (the most frequent colour is unique).
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of
%      all occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell
%      already contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/
%      column indices of cells coloured Cmax.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Domain information (grid size)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Compute the number of occurrences for each colour (non‑zero)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
count(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Choose the colour Cmax with maximal count
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% a) The maximal count value
maxCount(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { N : count(C,N) }.

% b) The colour(s) achieving that maximal count
best(Cmax) :- count(Cmax,Nmax), maxCount(Nmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Bounding box of Cmax
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_row(Rmin) :- best(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- best(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- best(Cmax), Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmaxcol) :- best(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Output exactly one colour per cell inside the bounding box
%    (the chosen colour is Cmax)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), best(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) Preserve original colour inside the bounding box
%    – a cell that originally had Cmax stays Cmax
%    – other cells become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 6) Safety: no two different colours for the same cell
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary fact to make best(Cmax) usable in later rules (optional)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- best(Cmax).
```

This forces Cmax to be a colour with maximal count. Good.

One more nuance: the rule `count(C,N)` also defines N for each color. This might cause multiple Nmax values? But `maxCount(Nmax)` uses #max across all count(C,N). This returns the maximum N among all colors. Then `best(Cmax)` ensures count(Cmax,Nmax) also yields same Nmax, forcing Cmax to be among the colors with that max count. rounds.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) Choose the colour Cmax that appears most often among
%      all non‑zero cells (the most frequent colour is unique).
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of
%      all occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell
%      already contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/
%      column indices of cells coloured Cmax.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Domain information (grid size)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Count non‑zero occurrences per colour
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
count(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Choose the colour Cmax with maximal count
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   a) the maximal count value
maxCount(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { N : count(C,N) }.

%   b) the colour(s) achieving that maximal count
best(Cmax) :- count(Cmax,Nmax), maxCount(Nmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Bounding box of the selected colour Cmax
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_row(Rmin) :- best(Cmax), Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- best(Cmax), Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
min_col(Cmin)  :- best(Cmax), Cmin  = #min { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.
max_col(Cmaxcol) :- best(Cmax), Cmaxcol = #max { C : input(_,C,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Output exactly one colour per cell inside the bounding box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) }
1 :- output_cell(R,C), best(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) Preserve the original colour inside the bounding box
%    – cells that originally had Cmax stay Cmax
%    – all other cells become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 6) Safety: no two different colours for the same cell
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary fact (makes best(Cmax) available for later use)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- best(Cmax).
```
rounds.program_before:
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   1) The colour that appears most often among all non‑zero cells
%      (call it Cmax) is selected.
%   2) The output grid is exactly the axis‑aligned bounding box of all
%      occurrences of Cmax.
%   3) Inside that box every cell gets Cmax iff the original cell already
%      contained Cmax; otherwise it gets 0.
%   4) The bounding box of Cmax is computed from the min/max row/column
%      indices of cells coloured Cmax.
%   5) The most frequent colour is unique, so the solution is forced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Domain information (grid size)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Colour predicate
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1) Choose a colour Cmax with maximal non‑zero count
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% a) Exactly one colour is selected (non‑zero)
1 { Cmax : color(Cmax), Cmax != 0 } 1 :- true.

% b) Count occurrences for any colour (non‑zero)
count_sel(C,N) :- color(C), C != 0, N = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% c) Ensure the selected colour has maximal count
:- count_sel(Cmax,Nmax),
   count_sel(D,N),
   D != Cmax,
   N > Nmax.

% d) Ensure the selected colour appears at least once
:- count_sel(Cmax,Nmax), Nmax > 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2) Bounding box of Cmax (derived from its occurrences)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
Rmin(Rmin) :- Cmax, Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Rmax(Rmax) :- Cmax, Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,Cmax) }.
Cmin(Cmin) :- Cmax, Cmin = #min { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.
Cmaxcol(Cmaxcol) :- Cmax, Cmaxcol = #max { I : input(_,I,Cmax) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax, C >= Cmin, C <= Cmaxcol.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3) Output exactly one colour per cell inside the box
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), output_cell(R,C) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4) Preserve original colour inside the bounding box
%    – a cell that originally had Cmax stays Cmax
%    – other cells become 0
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), input(R,C,Cmax), V = Cmax.
:- output(R,C,V), output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,Cmax), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5) Safety: no two colours for the same cell
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, output_cell(R,C).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   In every training example there is exactly one 3x3 sub‑grid
%   whose border (the 8 outer cells) all have the same colour.
%   This colour is the most frequent colour in the whole grid
%   and the centre of the sub‑grid may be either 0 or the same colour.
%   The task is to output that 3×3 block unchanged:
%       – the eight border cells keep the border colour,
%       – the centre cell keeps the colour it has in the input.
%   The program finds the unique 3×3 block that satisfies the
%   border condition, extracts the border colour (Bc) and the centre
%   colour (Vc) and writes exactly those nine cells to the output.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- description of a candidate 3×3 block ---------------------------
% Bc = border colour, Vc = colour of the centre cell.
border(Bc,R0,C0) :-
    input(R0,   C0,   Bc),
    input(R0,   C0+1, Bc),
    input(R0,   C0+2, Bc),
    input(R0+1, C0,   Bc),
    input(R0+1, C0+2, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0,   Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0+1, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0+2, Bc).

center(Vc,R0,C0) :-
    input(R0+1, C0+1, Vc).

block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R0 >= 0, C0 >= 0,
    R0+2 < H,   C0+2 < W,
    border(Bc,R0,C0),
    center(Vc,R0,C0).

% ----- write the nine output cells ------------------------------------
% border cells
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0,   C = C0,   V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0,   C = C0+1, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0,   C = C0+2, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+1, C = C0,   V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+1, C = C0+2, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+2, C = C0,   V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+2, C = C0+1, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+2, C = C0+2, V = Bc.

% centre cell
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+1, C = C0+1, V = Vc.

% ----- domain of the output -------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- output(R,C,_).

% ----- enforce a unique block and exactly nine output cells ----------
#count { R0,C0,Bc,Vc : block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc) } = 1.
#count { R,C : output_cell(R,C) } = 9.
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Count non-zero cells in a row

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

    input(R0+2, C0+2, Bc).

center(Vc,R0,C0) :-

block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc) :-
    height(H), width(W),

% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   In every training example there is exactly one 3×3 sub‑grid.
%   The eight cells on its border all have the same colour (the
%   “border colour”).  This colour is the most frequent colour in the
%   whole input grid.  The centre cell may be 0 or the same colour.
%   The required output is that 3×3 block unchanged:
%     – the eight border cells keep the border colour,
%     – the centre cell keeps the colour it has in the input.
%   The program therefore:
%     1. finds the unique 3×3 block whose border satisfies the
%        uniform‑colour condition,
%     2. records the border colour (Bc) and the centre colour (Vc),
%     3. writes exactly those nine cells to the output.
%   The constraints “#count … = 1” (exactly one block) and
%   “#count … = 9” (exactly nine output cells) guarantee a single,
%   correctly sized answer set.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- description of a candidate 3×3 block ---------------------------
border(Bc,R0,C0) :-
    input(R0,   C0,   Bc),
    input(R0,   C0+1, Bc),
    input(R0,   C0+2, Bc),
    input(R0+1, C0,   Bc),
    input(R0+1, C0+2, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0,   Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0+1, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0+2, Bc).

center(Vc,R0,C0) :-
    input(R0+1, C0+1, Vc).

block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R0 >= 0, C0 >= 0,
    R0+2 < H,   C0+2 < W,
    border(Bc,R0,C0),
    center(Vc,R0,C0).

% ----- write the nine output cells ------------------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0,   C = C0,   V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0,   C = C0+1, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0,   C = C0+2, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+1, C = C0,   V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+1, C = C0+2, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+2, C = C0,   V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+2, C = C0+1, V = Bc.
output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+2, C = C0+2, V = Bc.

output(R,C,V) :-
    block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc), R = R0+1, C = C0+1, V = Vc.

output_cell(R,C) :- output(R,C,_).

% ----- enforce uniqueness and exact size -------------------------------
#count { R0,C0,Bc,Vc : block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc) } = 1.
#count { R,C : output_cell(R,C) } = 9.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. ```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% compute max row and column indices
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(MC) :- MC = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% grid dimensions
height(H) :- max_row(MR), H = 1 + MR.
width(W)  :- max_col(MC), W = 1 + MC.

% block definition
border(Bc,R0,C0) :-
    input(R0, C0, Bc),
    input(R0, C0+1, Bc),
    input(R0, C0+2, Bc),
    input(R0+1, C0, Bc),
    input(R0+1, C0+2, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0+1, Bc),
    input(R0+2, C0+2, Bc).

center(Vc,R0,C0) :-
    input(R0+1, C0+1, Vc).

block(R0,C0,Bc,Vc) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R0 >= 0,
    C0 >= 0,
    R0+2 < H,
    C0+2 < W,
    border(Bc,R0,C0),
    center(Vc,R0,C0).

output(R,C,V) :-

selected_output(R,C,V) :- output(R,C,V), true.

<attempt_1>
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered from the ARC‑AGI examples
%
%   • The output consists of a 3×3 sub‑grid taken from the input.
%   • In that sub‑grid the three corners
%       (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) must contain the same
%       non‑zero colour.  The colour in the centre may be anything
%       (in the examples it is 0).
%   • Exactly one such 3×3 region exists for every training example;
%     the selected region is copied unchanged to the output grid,
%     which is always 3×3 and starts at (0,0).
%
%   The rule therefore: “find the unique 3×3 window whose three
%   non‑zero corners share a colour and output that window at the
%   origin of a new 3×3 grid.”
%
%   The ASP program below implements this rule:
%   – three_corner(R,C,Col)   : the three corners of the window
%     (R,C+2), (R+2,C), (R+2,C+2) have the same non‑zero colour.
%   – subgrid_possible(R,C)   : there exists such a colour.
%   – exactly one subgrid is selected.
%   – the nine cells of the selected 3×3 block are written to
%     output(0..2,0..2,Col) exactly as they appear in the input.
%
%   No aggregates, no division, and no unsafe predicates are used.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. The three corners that must share a colour (non‑zero)
three_corner(R,C,Col) :-
    input(R, C+2, Col),          % top‑right
    input(R+2, C, Col),          % bottom‑left
    input(R+2, C+2, Col),        % bottom‑right
    Col != 0.                    % we are interested in a real colour

% 2. A 3×3 window is possible at (R,C) iff the three corners
%    have a common non‑zero colour.
subgrid_possible(R,C) :-
    three_corner(R, C, Col).

% 3. Exactly one subgrid is selected.
%    (choose the window with the property above)
1 { subgrid(R,C) : true } 1 :- subgrid_possible(R,C).
%    (any other window that satisfies the property must not be chosen)
:- subgrid_possible(R,C), not subgrid(R,C).

% 4. Copy the selected 3×3 window to the output grid.
%    The output grid has coordinates (0,0) … (2,2).
output(0,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C,   V).
output(0,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C+1, V).
output(0,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C+2, V).
output(1,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C,   V).
output(1,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C+1, V).
output(1,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C+2, V).
output(2,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C,   V).
output(2,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+1, V).
output(2,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+2, V).

% End of program
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% Find the unique 3×3 window whose three corner cells
% (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) share a non‑zero colour.
% Copy that window unchanged to the output 3×3 grid (rows 0‑2,
% columns 0‑2). The whole program must admit exactly one answer set.

%    same non‑zero colour.
three_corner(R,C,Col) :-

    input(R+2, C,   Col),   % bottom‑left

subgrid(R,C) :- subgrid_possible(R,C).

output(2,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+2, V).

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Find the unique 3×3 window whose three corner cells
%    (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) share a non‑zero colour.
%  Copy that window unchanged to the output 3×3 grid (rows 0‑2,
%  columns 0‑2).  The whole program must admit exactly one answer set.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. The three corners of a candidate window (R,C) must have the
%    same non‑zero colour.
three_corner(R,C,Col) :-
    input(R,   C+2, Col),   % top‑right
    input(R+2, C,   Col),   % bottom‑left
    input(R+2, C+2, Col),   % bottom‑right
    Col != 0.

% 2. A 3×3 window is possible at (R,C) iff its three corners match.
subgrid_possible(R,C) :- three_corner(R,C,Col).

% 3. Define a subgrid fact for every possible window.
subgrid(R,C) :- subgrid_possible(R,C).

% 4. Exactly one such window must exist.
1 {R,C : subgrid_possible(R,C)} 1 :- subgrid_possible(R,C).

% 5. Copy the selected window to the output grid (0..2,0..2).
output(0,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C,   V).
output(0,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C+1, V).
output(0,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C+2, V).
output(1,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C,   V).
output(1,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C+1, V).
output(1,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C+2, V).
output(2,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C,   V).
output(2,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+1, V).
output(2,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+2, V).

% End of program
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% Find the unique 3×3 window whose three corner cells
% (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) share a non‑zero colour.
% Copy that window unchanged to the output 3×3 grid (rows 0‑2, columns 0‑2).
% Exactly one such window must exist.

% Three corners of a candidate window (R,C) with colour Col.
three_corner(R, C, Col) :-
    input(R, C + 2, Col),
    input(R + 2, C, Col),
    input(R + 2, C + 2, Col),
    Col != 0.

% A 3×3 window is possible at (R,C) iff its three corners match.
subgrid_possible(R, C) :- three_corner(R, C, Col).

% Define a subgrid fact for every possible window.
subgrid(R, C) :- subgrid_possible(R, C).

% Exactly one such window must exist.
1 { R, C } 1 :- subgrid(R, C).

% Copy the selected window to the output grid (0..2,0..2).
output(0, 0, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R,   C,   V).
output(0, 1, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R,   C + 1, V).
output(0, 2, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R,   C + 2, V).
output(1, 0, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 1, C,   V).
output(1, 1, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 1, C + 1, V).
output(1, 2, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 1, C + 2, V).
output(2, 0, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 2, C,   V).
output(2, 1, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 2, C + 1, V).
output(2, 2, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 2, C + 2, V).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Find the unique 3×3 window whose three corner cells
%    (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) share a non‑zero colour.
%  Copy that window unchanged to the output 3×3 grid (rows 0‑2,
%  columns 0‑2).  The whole program must admit exactly one answer set.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. The three corners of a candidate window (R,C) must have the
%    same non‑zero colour.
three_corner(R, C, Col) :-
    input(R, C + 2, Col),
    input(R + 2, C, Col),
    input(R + 2, C + 2, Col),
    Col != 0.

% 2. A 3×3 window is possible at (R,C) iff its three corners match.
subgrid_possible(R, C) :- three_corner(R, C, Col).

% 3. Define a subgrid fact for every possible window.
subgrid(R, C) :- subgrid_possible(R, C).

% 4. Exactly one such window must exist.
1 { R, C : subgrid_possible(R, C) } 1 :- subgrid_possible(R, C).

% 5. Copy the selected window to the output grid (0..2,0..2).
output(0, 0, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R,   C,   V).
output(0, 1, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R,   C + 1, V).
output(0, 2, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R,   C + 2, V).
output(1, 0, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 1, C,   V).

output(2, 1, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 2, C + 1, V).
output(2, 2, V) :- subgrid(R, C), input(R + 2, C + 2, V).

% End of program

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered from the ARC‑AGI examples
%
%   • The output consists of a 3×3 sub‑grid taken from the input.
%   • In that sub‑grid the three corners
%       (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) must contain the same
%       non‑zero colour.  The colour in the centre may be anything
%       (in the examples it is 0).
%   • Exactly one such 3×3 region exists for every training example;
%     the selected region is copied unchanged to the output grid,
%     which is always 3×3 and starts at (0,0).
%
%   The rule therefore: “find the unique 3×3 window whose three
%   non‑zero corners share a colour and output that window at the
%   origin of a new 3×3 grid.”
%
%   The ASP program below implements this rule:
%   – three_corner(R,C,Col)   : the three corners of the window
%     (R,C+2), (R+2,C), (R+2,C+2) have the same non‑zero colour.
%   – subgrid_possible(R,C)   : there exists such a colour.
%   – exactly one subgrid is selected.
%   – the nine cells of the selected 3×3 block are written to
%     output(0..2,0..2,Col) exactly as they appear in the input.
%
%   No aggregates, no division, and no unsafe predicates are used.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. The three corners that must share a colour (non‑zero)
three_corner(R,C,Col) :-
    input(R, C+2, Col),          % top‑right
    input(R+2, C, Col),          % bottom‑left
    input(R+2, C+2, Col),        % bottom‑right
    Col != 0.                    % we are interested in a real colour

% 2. A 3×3 window is possible at (R,C) iff the three corners
%    have a common non‑zero colour.
subgrid_possible(R,C) :-
    three_corner(R, C, Col).

% 3. Exactly one subgrid is selected.
%    (choose the window with the property above)
1 { subgrid(R,C) : true } 1 :- subgrid_possible(R,C).
%    (any other window that satisfies the property must not be chosen)
:- subgrid_possible(R,C), not subgrid(R,C).

% 4. Copy the selected 3×3 window to the output grid.
%    The output grid has coordinates (0,0) … (2,2).
output(0,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C,   V).
output(0,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C+1, V).
output(0,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C+2, V).
output(1,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C,   V).
output(1,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C+1, V).
output(1,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+1, C+2, V).
output(2,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C,   V).
output(2,1,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+1, V).
output(2,2,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R+2, C+2, V).

% End of program
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Find the unique 3×3 window whose three corner cells
%    (top‑right, bottom‑left, bottom‑right) share a non‑zero colour.
%  Copy that window unchanged to the output 3×3 grid (rows 0‑2,

% 1. The three corners of a candidate window (R,C) must have the
%    same non‑zero colour.
three_corner(R,C,Col) :-
    input(R,   C+2, Col),   % top‑right
    input(R+2, C,   Col),   % bottom‑left
    input(R+2, C+2, Col),   % bottom‑right

% 2. A 3×3 window is possible at (R,C) iff its three corners match.
subgrid_possible(R,C) :-

subgrid(R,C) :- subgrid_possible(R,C).

% 4. Exactly one such window must exist.
#count { R,C : subgrid_possible(R,C) } = 1.

% 5. Copy the selected window to the output grid (0..2,0..2).
output(0,0,V)  :- subgrid(R,C), input(R,   C,   V).

% End of program
